title: Age limit for lawyers commissioned as notaries
text:
HEADNOTE:
1. Occupational freedom entails both an economic dimension and a dimension related to the development of one’s personality. It gives definition to the fundamental right to the free development of one’s personality in the area of individual achievement and creating and earning a livelihood and aims to have the least amount of regulation of occupational activity possible. 2. Given the sustained shortage of lawyers commissioned as notaries and the current knowledge on how age affects professional faculties, the age limit of 70 years pursuant to § 47 no. 2, first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Notaries Act only achieves the objectives pursued by such rule – the proper functioning of the administration of justice, the (fair) distribution of career opportunities among generations and the protection of the administration of justice from risks that may arise from age-related decline in faculties among notaries – to a very limited extent. It disproportionately restricts occupational freedom insofar as it concerns lawyers commissioned as notaries.

SUMMARY:
I. The applicant – a lawyer commissioned as a notary public in North Rhine-Westphalia – inter alia , challenges the statutory rule in § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Notaries Act (hereinafter, the “Act”), according to which the office of notary public expires upon reaching the age limit of 70 years of age both for notaries practicing primarily as such and for lawyers commissioned as notaries. The applicant alleges that the age limit violates his occupational freedom under Art. 12.1 of the Basic Law. II. Based on the considerations below, the Federal Constitutional Court held that the constitutional complaint is well-founded insofar as it challenges the age limit rule. This limit is incompatible with Art. 12.1 of the Basic Law insofar as it relates to lawyers commissioned as notaries. The age limit disproportionately interferes with the occupational freedom of lawyers commissioned as notaries. The age limit nevertheless serves constitutionally legitimate objectives. It is intended to ensure an appropriate age structure for notaries public that serves the interest of the effective administration of justice. An age limit is also a means to provide an appropriate distribution of career opportunities among generations; this constitutes a legitimate objective for employment and social policy. Finally, it serves the legitimate objective of protecting the administration of justice from risks that may arise due to age-related diminution of function among notaries public. Despite changing factual circumstances, the age limit remains suitable and necessary under constitutional law to achieve the legislative objectives. However, the age limit is no longer proportionate in the strict sense with regard to lawyers commissioned as notaries. It places an unreasonable burden on the holders of fundamental rights. The intensity of the interference is severe. The age limit is a rule that affects the choice of occupation. Upon reaching this limit, the commission as notary public expires. As the decisive criteria is impossible to avoid, the holders of fundamental rights have no influence on the end of their career nor can they change it to suit their own circumstances. The law does not provide for exceptions. The interference affects both areas of protection guaranteed by occupational freedom, i.e. obtaining an economic livelihood and developing one’s personality. The interference is mitigated to some extent by the possibility for those that must forfeit their office due to the age limit to remain active in the same field as a substitute (Notarvertreter) or temporary (Notariatsverwalter) notary public or to resume their primary occupation as a lawyer. Nevertheless, the degree of mitigation is very limited. Seventy years is a substantially later age than the standard retirement age set by law at which civil servants and others may claim full retirement benefits. On its own, however, this does not result in a mitigation of the intensity of the interference. Lawyers commissioned as notaries public – much like professionals in higher positions in the civil service and other occupations requiring higher education – generally begin this occupation at a relatively late age. In addition, in looking at society as a whole, career and job application paths have become more flexible and interruptions in the progression from education to a career to retirement have become increasingly more common. This must be weighed against the interests of the common good, which also have considerable weight. The proper functioning of the administration of justice – namely, providing high quality notarial services carried out by competent, sufficiently experienced notaries of various ages – is of great importance both on an individual level and for society as a whole. Equally important is the social and employment policy objective of the legislator to provide a fair distribution of career opportunities among generations. These objectives also serve to give effect to the rights of potential notaries under Art. 12.1 of the Basic Law to enter into the profession. With regard to lawyers commissioned as notaries, the age limit provides a very limited contribution towards these objectives. Given the sustained shortage of lawyers commissioned as notaries and the current knowledge on how age influences professional competence, the age limit only achieves the objectives pursued by such rule to a very limited extent. The burdens imposed on the fundamental rights holders by the challenged rule are therefore not in reasonable proportion to the significantly reduced benefits to the common good. The rule imposed by § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Act is, to the extent it applies to lawyers commissioned as notaries, incompatible with the Constitution rather than void. The age limit will be temporarily kept in place until 30 June 2026. It may no longer be applied after that date.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION:
Languages: German; English (translation by the Court).

CROSS-REFERENCES:
Federal Constitutional Court: - 1 BvR 3017/09, 19.06. 2012, Entscheidungen des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGE (Official Digest) 131, 130, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2012:rs20120619.1bvr301709; - 1 BvR 2870/10, 05.01.2011, Second Chamber of the First Panel, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2011:rk20110105.1bvr287010; - 1 BvR 1581/91, 29.10.1992, Second Chamber of the First Panel; - 1 BvR 32/87, 21.06.1989, BVerfGE 80, 257.
